text: I usually have a cup of strong tea with a bit of sugar, along with some toast with marmalade. If I'm feeling peckish, perhaps an egg from our chooks in the backyard – we've got two hens who give us eggs every morning! Nothing too fancy, really, just something simple to start me going for the day.

I usually make myself a simple sandwich and soup, perhaps some leftovers from last night's dinner if I have any. If my grandkids come round, we might have a little picnic together, maybe even bring out an old favourite tea set and have a cuppa while we eat. It's always lovely having them visit! Most weeks I'm eating here at home anyway; too much fuss for me to go into town unless absolutely necessary.
I'm a bit of an early bird, even now that I'm retired. My usual bedtime is quite late, actually – 10 o'clock. It allows me enough restful sleep before waking up at 6:30 am sharp.

I believe a bit of self-care is essential, even at my age. For me, it's all about finding what works for me and sticking to it. In the morning, getting some fresh air and sunlight really helps set me up for the day – whether that's taking a walk round the block or simply sitting outside while having a cuppa before starting work. After a busy day, unwinding with a good book and maybe a nice warm bath or a relaxing tea leaves me feeling quite content. Of course, my grandkids always make sure to give me plenty of hugs and chat; that's priceless! Sometimes we have lovely Sunday roasts together which is another favourite part of the week. It's funny how simple pleasures like these little moments of relaxation become such big priorities in later life. As an older person, I think people forget that looking after oneself isn't selfish – it's necessary for being there for others, too. So, yes, I take my self-care seriously, albeit in my own gentle way.

I find networking is quite straightforward once you know who to talk to. As a former admin assistant, I had regular interactions with colleagues across different departments, so building relationships was relatively easy. We'd chat during breaks, grab lunch together, or discuss ongoing projects. The key was being genuinely interested in others, remembering names and faces, and offering support where needed. When we moved into more formalised environments like schools and surgeries, our networks became slightly more structured – meetings, training sessions, and departmental gatherings helped build connections among team members. It wasn't too complicated; everyone seemed keen to collaborate and share best practices. Nowadays, since retiring, my professional network has largely remained close-knit. Many of those people have gone on to pursue other careers, but we still meet occasionally for catch-ups over tea or coffee. Some of them're now retired themselves! Those informal encounters allow us to reminisce, swap advice, and generally keep each other updated on what's been going on in their lives. Really, effective networking boils down to showing genuine interest in others and taking an active part yourself. If someone takes the trouble to listen attentively and offer helpful insights, why wouldn't they be inclined to return the favour later on?

I find my best work is done during the morning, so I try to establish a regular routine starting from around 7:30 am. A strong cuppa tea always helps me settle into focus mode! Before I start any task, I make sure I have all necessary papers and documents organised, either on my desk or neatly filed away. Then I set specific targets for myself – usually three main ones for the day. Having these objectives in mind really helps me stay focused throughout the morning. When it gets towards midday, we tend to take an hour-long break together as a family, which gives everyone some fresh air and relaxation. During this downtime, our youngest grandchild loves playing outside while I catch up on some emails and messages. In the afternoon, if I'm feeling energetic, I might squeeze in another session of tidying up or tackling paperwork. However, I never push too hard; those afternoons can be quite sluggish for me. As soon as dinner is ready, I like to spend more quality time with the kids/grandkids before settling in for the night. The evenings are generally reserved for unwinding and preparing for bed – perhaps a bit of gentle reading before drifting off to sleep around midnight.

I'm still getting used to them, to be honest. My son is always telling me how wonderful they're revolutionising everything, but I find it all a bit overwhelming. Whenever we need to have a proper chat, he'll come round here so we can catch up face-to-face. But when grandkids want to speak to me while I'm out or busy in the garden, I suppose video call suits us both – though sometimes my image gets a bit fuzzy due to lighting!

To be honest, since retiring, I have found myself missing the daily routine a bit. At first, I thought I'd feel more free and relaxed without the pressures of work, but what I find is that having some sort of framework helps me stay focused and productive. I try setting personal objectives each morning before I start my day – simple ones like tidying the garden, responding to emails, maybe even tackling a particular project or task list. Having these specific goals gives me direction, which otherwise might lead to feeling aimless or getting caught up in whatever catches my eye. Plus, keeping an organisational system going reminds me of my former job where discipline was essential. It’s comforting familiarity that provides stability during those quieter periods. Of course, there are still moments of freedom now, too! But overall, giving myself something tangible to focus on has been helpful.

You know, when I was still working, I always made sure to set achievable targets and deadlines, so everyone knew what expected of them. But even in a job where there were lots of changes, we'd have regular meetings to discuss any adjustments needed. It was all very structured, which helped me cope with uncertainty. Nowadays, since retirement, my priority is enjoying life rather than meeting specific targets – although I do try to stay busy! My grandkids like having me available for various projects and activities, and our local community centre keeps me occupied too.

I think advocacy is essential, even though my style might be quite understated. As a former secretary, I was used to supporting others rather than drawing attention to myself. However, once I'm involved in something meaningful, like representing our local community centre or explaining changes to patients in the surgeries where I worked, I become more vocal about what I believe is necessary. When needed, I'd speak clearly and respectfully about concerns, whether it's addressing issues related to staffing shortages, improved facilities, or resource allocation. My colleagues appreciate my level-headedness and willingness to listen before jumping into arguments. While being assertive isn't always easy for me, knowing that speaking out could lead to positive outcomes makes all the difference. It helps me feel I am making a contribution, which motivates me to carry on advocating for those who need support.

I think the key is to take a deep breath, calm myself down, and try to put into words what I'm feeling. Sometimes, talking it out with someone who knows me well helps clarify my thoughts and feelings. If I'm still struggling to articulate how I'm feeling, writing something down can be really helpful too. It sounds simple, but getting everything down on paper seems to give me perspective and allows me to process my thoughts more clearly. And sometimes, taking a step back and giving people space can be exactly what I need – it gives everyone involved some breathing room to figure things out together.

I love spending time with my grandkids! During family gatherings, we usually have a lovely spread of food, and I always make sure there's something for everyone to eat. My son is very helpful, he brings his kids along too, so it's great for them to spend some quality time together. We chat about our lives, share stories, and sometimes even play games like Monopoly or cards. If we're near each other, we might go for a walk or take a drive out into nature – I adore being outdoors! Sometimes we'll watch a movie or two, and I'm quite particular about what I watch; usually, it's an older film, perhaps one of the classics. The young ones seem to enjoy whatever I choose, though. After all, it's wonderful having them here. At dinner, we talk about everything going on in their lives and listen to each other's concerns. It really feels like nothing else matters while we're all together. Those moments mean a lot to me.

I must admit, I'm not too keen on these modern platforms like LinkedIn. As a busy retiree, I find myself more focused on staying connected with my loved ones rather than building an online presence. When needed, I might join a Facebook page related to local issues or attend virtual meetings for charity work, but I wouldn't say I have much interest in creating content or networking professionally. My focus is keeping in touch with family and enjoying quieter hobbies.

I'd say I'm quite direct and honest when discussing difficult subjects, even though some people might find me a bit blunt. If there's something bothering them, I'll usually try to have an open conversation, explaining why my perspective is what I think is best, rather than sugarcoating or beating around the bush. It's essential to be heard and understood, isn't it? Nowadays, I make sure to consider other people's feelings before jumping into the discussion, so we're having constructive conversations where everyone feels valued. And if needed, I am happy to listen actively too!

I'd say I'm quite particular about what I consider acceptable behaviour among my peers, which might make me resistant to certain pressures at first. However, once we have a chance to discuss the issue openly and honestly, I think most people will find our conversation is more productive and helpful than being forced into something that doesn't feel right. I value fairness, kindness, and respect above all else; those principles guide how I interact with others. If someone's opinions clash, I'll certainly listen attentively, but I wouldn't compromise on core convictions unless there's strong reasoning behind an alternative viewpoint.

To be honest, I find those rather intrusive. If I'm browsing something on my laptop or using my son's tablet, he usually clears his cookies and wipes any browsing history afterwards so we're starting fresh each time. Occasionally, if an ad is really persistent, I'll make a note to myself to report them to Google or the relevant company. Don't think there's much one individual can do against these big corporations though!

I must admit, it's a bit of a struggle for me! To be honest, I'm still getting used to having all these digital tools available at my fingertips. My son helps me out sometimes, explaining how to sort them into folders and whatnot, but generally speaking, I tend to stick to only one or two main browsers open at any given time – usually Google Chrome and perhaps Safari for YouTube videos. It gets overwhelming otherwise, and I find myself clicking between windows more often than necessary. Sometimes I wish there was an easier way to organize everything, like some kind of simple system where all my frequently visited websites were grouped together... But, oh dear, I think I might need a refresher course on modern web management!

I try to ignore them whenever I can, but sometimes I have to take a stand and politely point out why what they're saying isn't acceptable. As someone who grew up with strong moral principles, I believe in standing up for myself and others, even if it might be uncomfortable. A simple "that kind of language is unacceptable" usually does the trick – I'm not one for getting into arguments, though! If all else fails, I'll block them or report abusive content where necessary.

When there's a trouble with our video call, my grandchild usually sorts out the issue – he's very handy with these modern contraptions! If we're having trouble getting started, we'll try restarting the device or checking the Wi-Fi connection together until it works again. Sometimes, if none of those steps work, I might ask him to walk me through what he'd like to say so we can communicate verbally while waiting for them to sort it out technically. It always seems to be worth persevering, because catching up is more important to us than being perfectly synchronized.

I like to think it's a combination of discipline and habit, really. Since my husband was always very good with numbers, he helped me sort out our finances before we got married. We stuck to a simple system where all our income goes into one account, which is then split between essential expenses like rent/mortgage, utilities, food, etc., savings, and discretionary spending. It makes sense to have everything separate so I know exactly how much I'm spending each month. We used to make regular statements every three months or six months, whichever comes first, to check our progress against what we had planned. If there were any issues, we'd adjust accordingly. When I lost my job due to illness during my late fifties, having an emergency fund covered two years' worth of living costs meant I didn't need to go cap-in-hand to the local council for assistance, thank goodness! Nowadays, since retirement, I take great pleasure in managing my state pension and other income streams – making sure I receive them regularly and investing wisely. My rule-of-thumb is "if it's not coming straight away, wait." There's nothing worse than overspending because something isn't arriving immediately. And, of course, keeping track of receipts helps too; a bit of

To be honest, I'm a bit of a hesitant one when it comes to cutting ties with subscriptions. Once something is set up, I figure why bother cancelling now? But my grandkids always tell me I should sort out my digital clutter too! They're always saying stuff like "Nana, how many streaming platforms do we really need?" And I suppose they have a point – I mean, I know most of them overlap anyway! If I haven't used something in months, I'll think twice before keeping it. It might be a magazine subscription I signed up for because someone thought it'd be a nice gesture, but never actually read... yeah, those usually go straight into the bin. On the other hand, if there's something I genuinely use regularly, even if it's just occasionally, I try to find an alternative option rather than getting rid of it altogether. It's all about striking a balance between convenience and being sensible with our money. My rule-of-thumb is to ask myself whether I could afford to spend more on another similar service or product instead. If not, I cut it loose. Simple as that! Now, where did I put my knitting needles again?

I'm a bit of a saver, even now that I'm retired. When considering a major purchase like replacing my old washing machine or booking a special outing, such as a coach trip to the Lake District, I think very carefully about whether it'll be worth spending money on. I set myself a budget and stick to it. My late husband always said "a penny saved is a penny earned," and I still live by those words. We had a frugal upbringing, so saving has been ingrained in me since childhood. Before making any decision, I weigh up all the costs involved – including running expenses and how much we'd save in the long run by buying something more efficient or modern. If it's going to make our lives easier, improve health, or bring us joy, then I consider it worthwhile investing. But if it's purely unnecessary, I try to resist the temptation.

I'm a definite saver, so I always try to think twice before making a purchase, whether it's something big like furniture or smaller items like kitchen appliances. When deciding between buying new and used, my first thought is usually how much wear and tear will the item endure, considering its intended use and maintenance requirements. If an item has been well-cared for and still looks brand new, I might consider purchasing second-hand – I find people tend to price them lower because there's already some depreciation done. On the other hand, if someone's offering me a significant discount for a nearly-new item that I know I'll be using frequently, such as a replacement for our old oven, I'd lean towards buying new; newer models have better features and safety standards these days. But generally speaking, unless it's essential, I opt for second-hand whenever possible, knowing it helps reduce waste and saves money. Plus, I rather enjoy hunting out hidden gems at charity shops, estate sales, or auctions!

Well, it's really a matter of being mindful of one's spending habits and sticking to what we call "the rule of thumb" – waste not, want not! My late husband always said it was essential to live below our means and save for the future. When he passed away, his words stuck with me, and I made sure to carry them forward. We used to be quite thrifty, even before my retirement, so it wasn't too difficult to adjust once again. I try to remember every single penny and make sure there's enough set aside each month. I'm not extravagant; I know how to have fun without breaking the bank. For instance, instead of buying something brand-new, I'll look out for second-hand items or take advantage of shop sales. It might seem simple, but trust me, it makes all the difference! Nowadays, I find myself scrutinising receipts like an accountant! A bit old-fashioned, perhaps, but it helps me identify where I could cut back elsewhere to free up some extra dosh for savings. And, of course, I never forget to put money into our emergency fund, which is vital for those unpredictable moments. All these little tricks have helped us build a safety net and allowed us to stay debt-free.

I make a mental note of them and try to pop into my local stores during those times to snag some bargains. I'm quite particular about what I buy myself, so I tend to stick to essentials like linens, cleaning supplies, and maybe a few extra bits for the grandkids. My husband used to say I was always prepared because we'd plan our shopping trips ahead of time; now I find myself still being rather diligent about keeping an eye out for discounts!

I'm a firm believer in moderation. When cooking for myself or hosting guests, I try to balance my love of food with being mindful of what I consume. It's all about enjoying meals while keeping an eye on serving sizes. You know, like my grandmother used to say, "a little bit is nice, too much isn't." I find that having a slice of cake or two during dinner is perfectly fine; it's all part of sharing and taking pleasure in each other's company. Of course, there will be occasions where I need to cut back – perhaps after a big meal or when I have specific dietary restrictions – but overall, I think everything in moderation works best for me.

I think a face-to-face meeting is best for me. I'm someone who likes being able to read body language and tone of voice, which helps me pick up on how others feel. A therapist sitting across from me seems more personal and trusting than talking on the phone or via computer screen. Plus, I like having paper notes and jotting down thoughts afterwards – it makes sense to me. Of course, some people might prefer different approaches, but for my money, there's nothing like chatting with someone in a quiet room together.

Preventing burnout is a must for me, dear. You know, all those years spent juggling multiple jobs and responsibilities took its toll, so now I make sure to pace myself quite nicely. My advice is to prioritize your downtime – take breaks throughout the day whenever you need them, even if it's just taking a short walk outside or practicing some gentle stretches. And, of course, getting enough sleep is crucial; I aim for an early night, like I said before bed at ten o'clock. It really helps regulate my body clock. I find setting realistic expectations too helps avoid feeling overwhelmed. Don't be too hard on yourself, darling! Recognise what you're capable of managing each day, and only tackle what you can handle. Learning to say "no" is essential, too – whether it's politely decline invitations that might interfere with your personal time or refuse additional workloads that could lead to burnout. Oh, and staying hydrated, eating nutritious food, and exercising regularly will also support your mental wellbeing. A brisk morning stroll usually gets me energised for the rest of the day, which sets a lovely tone. Keeping busy hobbies and passions engaging is vital – mine includes tending to my garden and indulging in a bit of reading or baking. These

Well, I think getting a bit more regular with my bedtime is really key, isn't it? You know, going to bed at the same time every night helps me wind down properly and gets into a rhythm. And making sure I'm relaxing before bed too – perhaps having a cup of herbal tea or some calming music plays a part in unwinding. Although sometimes it feels like modern technology can be quite stimulating, so turning off all electronic devices an hour beforehand does make a difference. I've found it easier lately since retiring, not feeling rushed during the day. Still need to remind myself though; sometimes those mid-afternoon dips catch me out! A nice walk outside usually sorts me out, even if it's just round the block. As I said, I value routine and consistency, which seems to work for me. Getting enough exercise during the day helps too – nothing strenuous mind, just something gentle, like stretching. Now, if only I could remember where I put my slippers...

I like to mix it up a bit to avoid boredom and prevent injury. Most days, I stick to my usual morning walk round the block – about an hour should be enough. But sometimes, I'll take the grandkids out for a bike ride along the canal path; we have lovely views of the countryside. When it's nice weather, I might spend some time tending to my vegetable patch – nothing too strenuous, just getting the soil ready, pruning plants... It helps me unwind, actually. And during the winter months, I join the local community centre gym for some gentle yoga classes – that's always helpful when the cold weather sets in! Nothing too intense, mind you, I'm 70 now and need to listen to my body.

I think I'd always try to weigh up the pros and cons, really consider what each option might bring and how it'll affect my daily life and priorities. For me, simplicity is key – I like knowing exactly where I stand and having a plan. If there's too much complexity or uncertainty, it gets overwhelming. But if something seems sensible, logical, and benefits either myself or others, I'm more likely to give it a go. Of course, I have to be pragmatic – sometimes we just need to make do with what we have, rather than chasing perfection. And I suppose, ultimately, it comes down to trusting my instincts and making decisions based on common sense.

I think incorporating feedback is quite crucial. When someone takes the trouble to share their thoughts or concerns, it shows they're invested in a particular issue. If we take the time to listen attentively and acknowledge what they have said, it helps them feel heard and understood. Then, when considering our response, we should ask ourselves why they felt that way and how we might be able to address those specific issues. Sometimes it comes down to simple changes like rephrasing something or offering alternatives. Other times, though, we need more substantial adjustments – perhaps revisiting a decision-making process or involving others who could offer valuable insights. By being open to constructive feedback, we demonstrate respect for other people's opinions and show that we value our relationships built upon mutual trust. It really makes all the difference!

I think my approach to analytical thinking is quite methodical, actually. When faced with a complex issue or problem, I like to break it down into smaller, more manageable parts and examine each component individually before looking at how they fit together. It helps me identify patterns and connections that might be hard to spot otherwise. It's rather like tending to my garden – one must first assess the soil conditions, prune any dead bits, and plant seeds for future growth. You wouldn't start building an entire greenhouse overnight! Similarly, when tackling a puzzle, I try to consider all available information, evaluate different scenarios, and make informed decisions based on evidence. Of course, sometimes there may be multiple paths forward, but having a structured thought process makes it easier to weigh options and choose the best route. Plus, being someone who enjoys reading history and biographies has honed my ability to look at big picture contexts and appreciate how past influences present-day issues. A bit of perspective never hurts, does it? Overall, my aim is always to arrive at a thoughtful solution, considering various viewpoints and facts along the way.

I find that writing things down really helps me sort out my thoughts. If something's been bothering me or I'm unsure about a particular issue, I'll jot down some notes and let them sit overnight. In the morning, I re-read what I wrote and usually have a clearer idea of where I stand. It's amazing how much mental clutter clears once you put pen to paper! Sometimes, too, I'll ask myself "what's the real reason" – why am I feeling a certain way or worrying about something. Asking those kinds of questions seems to help untangle my thoughts.

I think identifying areas for growth is all about being honest with oneself, isn't it? For me, it starts with reflection – sitting quietly with a cuppa and thinking about what's been going on lately. Is there something that's causing frustration or feeling like it's holding me back? Maybe it's my reaction to certain situations or people. One thing I've come across recently is how much more tech-savvy I could be, even though I'm still getting frustrated with these new-fangled gadgets! My grandsons seem to pick them up so effortlessly; I suppose it's because they're growing up with it. Perhaps having some lessons or guided tutorials might make all the difference? Another area where I feel like I'd love to improve is probably staying mentally sharp. As we age, I know our brains aren't quite what they used to be. So maybe engaging in crosswords or brain teasers, playing games, or participating in intellectually stimulating conversations would really help. Lastly, I think taking better care of myself physically has always slipped into the background, which can lead to burnout. Getting enough rest, eating healthily, exercising regularly... those sorts of basics need constant tending to, especially now that I'm in retirement. Oh, and one last thought

I think my strategy is quite simple, really. It's all about being open to the idea of change, even if it makes me feel a bit uncomfortable initially. You know, I'm an ambivert, so I like meeting people and enjoying group conversations, but I also value my alone time and can be happy spending a quiet afternoon reading or tending to my garden. When faced with something new, I try to focus on why it might be helpful or interesting rather than worrying about how awkward it'll make me feel. And I always ask myself, "Is there someone who could teach me more about this?" If I find out I need guidance or support, I wouldn't hesitate to reach out. It's funny, isn't it? As we age, some folks seem to become set in their ways, resistant to change. But not me! I believe that staying curious and flexible helps us adapt better to the twists and turns life throws our way. Of course, it's nice to have tried-and-tested methods, too – sometimes a little familiarity can give me peace of mind. Still, I'd much rather take calculated risks and discover what works best for me. After all, as I say, "patience and curiosity" go far in navigating uncharted territory

I make sure to set aside a dedicated block of time each morning before my daily routine gets busy. It usually starts around 7:30 am, while still having my cuppa, and lasts till about 9:30 am. My mind is fresher then too, so I can really focus on getting lost in a story or absorbing some interesting facts. Even though my eyesight isn't what it used to be, I manage all right thanks to those lovely spectacles hanging round my neck! Now, I must say, sometimes I'll squeeze in an extra chapter during me tea break later in the afternoon, but generally speaking, the bulk of me reading happens first thing.

I think I discovered my love for historical fiction while browsing through an old bookstore in our village. The owner had a wonderful section dedicated to classic novels, and one author's writing style really caught my eye – Elizabeth Gaskell, I believe. Her writing was so vivid and immersive; I couldn't put her books down! After that, I started exploring more authors like Wilkie Collins and Anthony Trollope. As for discovering newer authors, I usually ask my book club members what they're currently enjoying. We have such lively discussions afterwards, and someone always recommends something fantastic. Occasionally, I'll stumble upon articles or reviews in newspapers or magazines that pique my interest, which leads me to try out a new author or genre. And sometimes, if a grandchild suggests a title, I give them credit for introducing me to another great writer!

I love having a bit of music playing in the background while I'm doing various chores or just relaxing. It really helps me focus and unwind. Sometimes, I'll be tidying up my garden, pruning plants or watering them, and some calming classical music will match the pace perfectly. Other times, I might be sewing or knitting, and soft instrumental tunes suit those gentle activities nicely too. Occasionally, if I'm ironing clothes or cooking something, upbeat pop songs come on and get me moving along – nothing like a catchy tune to make an hour fly by! Music has always been a comforting companion, hasn't changed much since I was young; Mum used to say I'd hum along even before there were any lyrics – still is true today!

I find I'm rather fond of them, actually! There's something comforting about being able to listen while still having my hands free, whether it's during a walk or whilst tending to some domestic chores. Audiobooks have been really helpful since my husband passed away – we used to love sitting together watching history documentaries, now I do it myself. Of course, there's nothing like getting lost in a good story, be it an audiobook or podcast, though I prefer one where someone else does the talking, personally. It saves me so much time!

I'm rather fond of getting out into my garden whenever I have a bit of free time. There's something therapeutic about digging in the soil, watching those seeds sprout, and tending to all sorts of plants. It's been a lifelong passion of mine, really. In fact, most weekdays morning is usually spent there, weather permitting. It's lovely exercise too – gets me out of the house before the rest of the world wakes up! Occasionally, I'll join some fellow villagers for a spot of gardening or we might attend local community centre events together; nothing too strenuous, mind you. When I need a break from being outdoors, I enjoy curling up with a good book, preferably an engaging history novel or biography. And let's not forget baking – whipping up a treat for my grandkids always puts a smile on everyone's face, including mine!

I find solace in having some me-time, actually. It gives me a chance to recharge my batteries, sort out my thoughts, and pursue hobbies like gardening or reading without any distractions. There's something peaceful about being surrounded by nature, watching plants grow, or getting lost in a good story. Of course, with grandkids coming over all the time now, I cherish every moment we spend together too! But when I'm on my own, I really appreciate the freedom to take a deep breath and be myself, without anyone else influencing what I think or feel.

I love trying new foods, even though I must admit my adventurous streak is a bit more tempered now that I'm older! When I try something completely foreign, like an Indian curry or Japanese sushi, I'll usually start with bits I know will be safe – perhaps some chicken tikka masala or a simple salad before venturing into unfamiliar dishes. My son says his wife does similar when cooking us meals; we all tend to ease ourselves into new flavours gradually! If someone invites me out to dinner somewhere I haven't tried before, I always ask what sort of cuisine they're serving. If I feel uncertain, I might politely decline until another opportunity arises, unless there's been rave reviews about it beforehand. Of course, sometimes surprises are lovely too! We have wonderful local restaurants here near our village where everything seems so fresh and made-to-order... Give me a warm, comforting bowl of homemade soup any day, really. Still, every little bit counts, and discovering hidden gems nearby brings great joy.

I think that's a very important aspect of staying happy and fulfilled, don't you? For me, creating variety is all about balancing different activities to suit my moods and preferences. In the morning, I love getting out into my garden – tending to my vegetables and flowers always brings me so much joy. But then, come afternoons, I find myself craving something more stimulating. So, I'll join our local book club or volunteer at the community centre. These little breaks give me a chance to meet people, catch up on current events, and exercise my brain in different ways. At other times, like during the evenings, I might indulge in some relaxing hobbies like knitting or watching nature documentaries. It really depends on how I'm feeling, what I need to unwind or be inspired. And of course, spending time with my grandkids is always a treat! Whether we're baking together, playing board games, or simply having tea and chat, those moments never fail to put a smile on my face. All these varied activities keep life interesting and make sure I feel content.

I like to think of myself as a bit of an organised soul, even though I'm not always as tidy as I should be! *laughs* When it comes to documenting my leisure activities, such as gardening or writing, I tend to jot down notes in a notebook or diary. It helps me remember what I've done, how I got there, and any tips or observations I might have made along the way. Sometimes, I'll make some rough sketches of plants or diagrams of my garden layout – nothing too fancy, mind you! For bigger projects, like planning a weekend trip to visit the grandkids, I'll put together a little folder with all the relevant documents: train tickets, accommodation bookings, maps... You know, just so everything is in one place and easy to find later. And of course, I love making lists – who doesn't adore a good list, eh?! *smiles* As for keeping track of my hobbies specifically, I actually found it really helpful to set aside dedicated blocks of time each month to focus on whatever activity I was enjoying most. Whether that's tending to my roses or working on a puzzle, having those specific times carved out makes sure I prioritize them. Plus, seeing progress and tracking my accomplishments gives me a lovely sense

I'm a bit old-fashioned, but I still believe in keeping records and notes in a system. For me, it starts with writing everything down – appointments, reminders, birthdays...you name it! My calendar is always full, even though my son has since taken over managing most of mine these days. As for files, I have separate ones for work and personal documents. Everything gets filed away neatly once it's sorted out. Don't know what anyone does nowadays, all this digital storage and cloud-sharing, but there's something reassuring about having papers and folders tidy. Occasionally, I'll scan important documents so they're easier to access digitally too. My daily planner helps keep track of tasks and chores. It's an old habit formed during my secretarial days; can be very helpful. Although, I must admit, sometimes I forget where I put certain bits of paper! Still works quite well, considering its age. Lastly, maintaining routines really helps me feel more organized. Getting up every morning at 6:30 am gives me plenty of time to tackle any pressing tasks before the rest of the world wakes up. Plus, a spot of tea never hurts either! It might sound simple, but simplicity suits me best. Just need some sort of framework to fall into,

I like starting my morning with a nice cup of tea and a bit of time to myself while getting ready. Before heading out, I usually take some deep breaths and plan out what I need to accomplish during the day. Sometimes, I'll read something interesting or listen to music – I find it helps me feel more awake and focused. If I have any specific tasks lined up, such as running errands or meeting someone, I make sure to double-check everything is packed and prepared beforehand. It's always lovely having a sense of being organised!

I try to make sure we're using our heating system wisely during the winter months. We have a boiler, which is quite efficient, and I always check the thermostat before turning it up or down. In fact, my late husband used to say, "Turn it off when you leave the room!" Still chuckle about that one now. Also, since moving into this lovely little house, I've made an effort to draught-proof where I can – sealed any gaps around doors and windows, got some thermal curtains to help keep warm air in. It may seem like small things, but every bit counts! Simple habits like those make all the difference.

To be honest, I'm still getting used to them all. My grandkids got me a smart speaker thingy, which is lovely, really helps with reminders and whatnot. But I have to admit, sometimes I find it all a bit overwhelming. The menus are so complicated! And I know there's supposed to be voice control, but my ears aren't what they used to be... Still, it does make life easier, like having someone else set alarms for me. I think I'll stick with it, though. It's nice to have some assistance with modernising, even if it takes some time getting used to. Would love some guidance on how to work it properly, mind – maybe one of those tech-savvy young people could show me the ropes!

Well, I think prioritising is all about weighing what's most important to me, isn't it? For instance, my grandkids always come first, so making sure I spend enough time with them is a top priority. But I'm also keen on keeping myself fit and healthy, which includes regular exercise and eating properly – that comes second. Of course, I have bills to pay and need to manage my finances sensibly, so those responsibilities follow fairly close behind. And I suppose one should never forget the importance of tending to my garden; that brings me such joy! It might seem simple, but really, getting everything done efficiently is key, you know? Once those basics are sorted, there's more room for enjoying hobbies like reading and spending time with dear friends.
